event: Paleo-Indian hunter-gatherers occupy the region, marking the earliest known human presence in present-day Belize.
Early Maya communities such as Cuello establish permanent farming villages, beginning the long Preclassic occupation.
Maya populations grow with maize agriculture, pottery, and trade networks spreading across the lowlands.
Maya ceremonial centers like Lamanai and Cerros develop monumental architecture and regional trade.
The Classic Maya period begins, with city-states such as Caracol and Xunantunich rising to prominence.
Caracol defeats the powerful city of Tikal, marking the height of Belizean Maya political and military influence.
The Classic Maya collapse leaves many great cities abandoned, though sites like Lamanai remain occupied for centuries.
Christopher Columbus sails along the Bay of Honduras during his fourth voyage, the first European contact with the region.
Shipwrecked English sailors and buccaneers begin settling the coast to cut logwood, founding the earliest European settlement.
The Treaty of Madrid between England and Spain seeks to curb piracy, indirectly affecting the British logwood cutters known as Baymen.
British Baymen expand logwood cutting operations despite Spanish claims of sovereignty over the territory.
The Treaty of Paris grants British settlers the right to cut logwood while affirming Spanish sovereignty over the land.
The Treaty of Versailles confirms British logwood-cutting rights and defines settlement boundaries between the Hondo and Belize rivers.
The Convention of London extends British cutting rights to mahogany, deepening a timber economy reliant on enslaved Africans.
The Baymen and their enslaved laborers defeat a Spanish naval force at the Battle of St. George's Caye, securing British control.
Slavery is fully abolished following emancipation across the British Empire, ending forced labor in the timber camps.
The Caste War of Yucatán drives thousands of Maya and Mestizo refugees south into northern British Honduras, reshaping its population.
Britain and Guatemala sign a boundary treaty, the source of a territorial dispute that persists into the modern era.
The settlement is formally declared the colony of British Honduras, administered under the Governor of Jamaica.
British Honduras becomes a Crown Colony with its own legislative council under direct British rule.
British Honduras is separated administratively from Jamaica and given its own governor.
A devastating hurricane strikes Belize Town, killing more than a thousand people and destroying much of the capital.
Economic hardship during the Great Depression sparks labor unrest and the rise of an early nationalist and workers' movement.
The People's United Party is founded under George Price, launching the modern movement for self-government and independence.
A new constitution introduces universal adult suffrage, and the People's United Party wins the colony's first elections under it.
Hurricane Hattie devastates Belize City, prompting plans to build a new inland capital.
British Honduras attains full internal self-government, with George Price becoming the first Premier.
The newly built city of Belmopan becomes the official capital, replacing the hurricane-prone Belize City.
The colony is renamed Belize, reflecting a growing national identity ahead of independence.
Belize secures United Nations support affirming its right to independence and territorial integrity against Guatemalan claims.
Belize gains full independence from the United Kingdom on 21 September, with George Price as its first Prime Minister.
Belize is admitted as a member state of the United Nations shortly after achieving independence.
The United Democratic Party wins the first post-independence general election, with Manuel Esquivel becoming Prime Minister.
Guatemala formally recognizes Belize's independence, easing decades of territorial tension.
British military forces withdraw from Belize, ending the long-standing defense guarantee against Guatemala.
The Belize Barrier Reef Reserve System is inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site, recognizing its global ecological importance.
Said Musa leads the People's United Party to a landslide election victory and becomes Prime Minister.
Belize and Guatemala begin an Organization of American States process to seek a peaceful resolution to their border dispute.
Dean Barrow becomes Belize's first Black Prime Minister after the United Democratic Party wins the general election.
Belize bans offshore oil drilling in its territorial waters to protect the barrier reef ecosystem.
UNESCO removes the Belize Barrier Reef from its List of World Heritage in Danger after major conservation gains.
Belizean voters approve in a referendum taking the territorial dispute with Guatemala to the International Court of Justice.
John Briceño leads the People's United Party to victory, becoming Prime Minister amid the COVID-19 pandemic's economic impact.
Belize completes a landmark debt-for-nature swap, restructuring its sovereign debt to fund marine conservation.
